title: Interactively Generating Explanations for Transformer Language Models

structured.summary: The paper introduces Proto-Trex, a prototype network for transformer language models, aimed at enhancing interpretability by providing case-based explanations directly integrated into the model. While it shows comparable performance with baseline models, it introduces an interactive component for users to refine model explanations, albeit with limited details on implementation and evaluation.

strengths.point: Introduction of prototype networks to Transformer LMs for explanations.
strengths.evidence: The paper proposes Prototypical-Transformer Explanation (Proto-Trex) Networks to provide prototypical examples for model predictions.
The paper introduces an interactive component, iProto-Trex, allowing user feedback to refine explanations.

weaknesses.evidence: The paper claims the use of prototypes as novel but similar approaches are cited from existing work in computer vision and sequential data (Li et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019).
The paper states Proto-Trex networks perform 'on par' with existing models without clear evidence of substantial benefits (Abstract).
The paper lacks detailed performance metrics or comparative analysis against a range of interpretability methods (Experimental Results section).

weaknesses.point: Vague claims on reduction of bias.
weaknesses.evidence: The abstract claims reduction of bias, but lacks empirical evidence or methodology demonstrating this claim.

weaknesses.point: Limited detailing of interactive learning setup.
weaknesses.evidence: The specifics on how user feedback influences model adjustments are not described sufficiently (Methods section on iProto-Trex).
The paper states 'promising results with learned prototypes providing helpful explanations' without defining what constitutes a helpful explanation.

weaknesses.point: Lack of context for prototype layer implementation.
weaknesses.evidence: Details on the prototype layer's implementation, such as initialization and updates during training, are missing (Proto-Trex Networks section).
No consideration of the scalability of the proposed method when applied to large datasets is discussed, despite being important for NLP applications.
While the Proto-Trex loss function is described, there is no rationale or evidence on how each loss component contributes to model performance or interpretability (Proto-Trex Loss section).
The manuscript contains typographical errors and formatting inconsistencies, such as 'I NTERACTIVELY G ENERATING E XPLANATIONS FOR' in the title.

weaknesses.point: Lack of human subject studies for explanation validation.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper lacks user studies or qualitative feedback mechanisms to validate the improvement in explanations promised through user interaction.
While practical applications are mentioned, the experiments do not cover real-world datasets or environments, focusing only on synthetic or standard datasets.

questions.question: How exactly do user interactions refine prototype explanations in iProto-Trex?
questions.motivation: Understanding the process and impact of user feedback is crucial for assessing the practicality and effectiveness of interactive learning setups.

questions.question: What metrics or studies were used to evaluate the quality of explanations?
questions.motivation: Determining the effectiveness of Proto-Trex's explanations necessitates concrete and well-defined evaluation metrics or studies.

questions.question: How does the model handle scalability and efficiency on large datasets?
questions.motivation: Scalability is essential for practical applications of the model on industrial-sized datasets typical in NLP tasks.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of novelty.. The paper claims the use of prototypes as novel but similar approaches are cited from existing work in computer vision and sequential data (Li et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019)
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient justification for prototype integration.. The paper states Proto-Trex networks perform 'on par' with existing models without clear evidence of substantial benefits (Abstract)
critique_points.point_003: Lack of rigorous comparative evaluation.. The paper lacks detailed performance metrics or comparative analysis against a range of interpretability methods (Experimental Results section)
critique_points.point_004: Vague claims on reduction of bias.. The abstract claims reduction of bias, but lacks empirical evidence or methodology demonstrating this claim
critique_points.point_005: Limited detailing of interactive learning setup.. The specifics on how user feedback influences model adjustments are not described sufficiently (Methods section on iProto-Trex)
critique_points.point_006: Ambiguity in explanation evaluation.. The paper states 'promising results with learned prototypes providing helpful explanations' without defining what constitutes a helpful explanation
critique_points.point_007: Lack of context for prototype layer implementation.. Details on the prototype layer's implementation, such as initialization and updates during training, are missing (Proto-Trex Networks section)
critique_points.point_008: Missing discussion on scalability.. No consideration of the scalability of the proposed method when applied to large datasets is discussed, despite being important for NLP applications
critique_points.point_009: Incomplete explanation of loss function.. While the Proto-Trex loss function is described, there is no rationale or evidence on how each loss component contributes to model performance or interpretability (Proto-Trex Loss section)
critique_points.point_010: Clarity and presentation issues.. The manuscript contains typographical errors and formatting inconsistencies, such as 'I NTERACTIVELY G ENERATING E XPLANATIONS FOR' in the title
critique_points.point_011: Lack of human subject studies for explanation validation.. The paper lacks user studies or qualitative feedback mechanisms to validate the improvement in explanations promised through user interaction
critique_points.point_012: Absence of real-world application scenarios.. While practical applications are mentioned, the experiments do not cover real-world datasets or environments, focusing only on synthetic or standard datasets
